museum.name: Gaming Time Machine
museum.dek: E.T. reaches the Atari 2600, the Vectrex and 5200 sit in the Christmas conversation, Time Pilot closes the arcade year, and British homes weigh computers against cartridges.
museum.curatorNote: December is tempting to read entirely through the later crash narrative. This exhibit keeps hindsight in the notes and tries to preserve what the month could have felt like from inside 1982: crowded, expensive, exciting and only partly anxious.

sections.title: News
sections.subtitle: December turns the 1982 boom into a shopping-season exhibit.

items.title: E.T. reaches Atari 2600 shelves
items.body: Atari's film tie-in arrived in time for Christmas after an infamously compressed development cycle. Its later reputation is heavy, but in December it was also a licensed object tied to one of the year's biggest films.
items.sourceNote: Computing History and E.T. references list December 1982 for the Atari 2600 release.

items.title: Vectrex enters its first Christmas
items.body: The vector console's built-in screen made it a spectacular present idea, but also an expensive and unusual one.
items.sourceNote: Vectrex launched in November 1982; December context is first holiday season.

items.title: Atari 5200 and ColecoVision compete for the premium shelf
items.body: American console buyers faced a new high-end comparison: Atari's successor machine, Coleco's arcade-conversion pitch, and the still-dominant 2600 library.
items.sourceNote: Atari 5200 and ColecoVision launched earlier in 1982; December context is seasonal.

items.title: UK families consider the home micro as Christmas machine
items.body: In Britain, a ZX Spectrum, BBC Micro, Dragon 32, VIC-20 or Commodore machine could be sold as education, hobby and games machine in one box.
items.sourceNote: UK microcomputer launch-year context.

items.title: The arcade year ends still bright
items.body: Pole Position, Q*bert, Joust, Dig Dug and Time Pilot made the arcade feel energetic at year's end, even as the home-console market was becoming crowded.
items.sourceNote: Based on verified 1982 arcade release anchors.

sections.title: Releases
sections.subtitle: Eight December-window entries and seasonal arrivals, with later crash mythology kept at a respectful distance.

items.title: E.T. the Extra-Terrestrial
items.body: A major film licence converted into a rushed Atari cartridge for the Christmas market.
items.sourceNote: Computing History lists December 1982; E.T. release references support the month.

items.title: Time Pilot
items.body: Konami's circular dogfighting shooter is commonly listed as a December 1982 arcade release.
items.sourceNote: GameSpot lists first release as December 1982; MobyGames lists 1982.

items.title: Pole Position
items.body: Fresh from its late-November North American release, Namco and Atari's racer became one of the arcade machines to watch at year's end.
items.sourceNote: North American release listed as November 30, 1982.

items.title: Mine Storm
items.body: The built-in game that many new Vectrex owners would have met first during the holiday season.
items.sourceNote: Mine Storm was built into the November 1982 Vectrex.

items.title: Super Breakout
items.body: The 5200 pack-in still defined many first encounters with Atari's premium console.
items.sourceNote: Atari Archive launch-window context.

items.title: Q*bert
items.body: By December, Gottlieb's pyramid-hopping cabinet belonged to the visible late-1982 arcade wave.
items.sourceNote: October publication and November AMOA context; December included as public-floor momentum.

items.title: Pitfall!
items.body: A September release that made enormous sense as a Christmas cartridge: original, readable, and demonstrably better than many older home games.
items.sourceNote: September 1982 release; December sales context not tied to one date.

items.title: Spectrum games on cassette
items.body: UK owners and gift-givers were entering a world where a game might be a tape, a listing, or a mail-order promise.
items.sourceNote: No precise December title date asserted.

sections.title: Hardware
sections.subtitle: The Christmas shelf split into console, cabinet and computer futures.

items.title: Atari 2600
items.body: Still the dominant home-console presence, now carrying both beloved originals like Pitfall! and overextended licences like E.T.
items.sourceNote: Anchored to Pitfall! and E.T. 1982 releases.

items.title: Vectrex
items.body: A beautiful oddity for households that wanted the arcade glow without surrendering the television.
items.sourceNote: Vectrex release sources.

items.title: Atari 5200 and ColecoVision
items.body: The high-end American console comparison was now a Christmas talking point: Atari power and brand weight against Coleco's Donkey Kong argument.
items.sourceNote: 1982 launch context for both platforms.

items.title: ZX Spectrum, BBC Micro, Dragon 32 and C64
items.body: In the UK, the computer could be presented as practical and educational while quietly promising games, programming and late-night cassette loading.
items.sourceNote: UK 1982 home micro context.

sections.title: Magazine Covers
sections.subtitle: December magazines were buying guides, wish lists and maps of an overcrowded future.

items.title: Christmas cartridge pressure
items.headline: Which game earns its place under the tree?
items.coverImageAlt: Reconstructed Electronic Games cover for December 1982.
items.curatorNote: The late-1982 console shelf was rich enough to excite and crowded enough to confuse.
items.body: The late-1982 console shelf was rich enough to excite and crowded enough to confuse.
items.sourceNote: Specific cover line not asserted.

items.title: C&VG festive arcade/home mix
items.headline: Arcades, micros and Christmas choices
items.curatorNote: UK readers needed a magazine that could move between Pole Position and the Spectrum without blinking.
items.body: UK readers needed a magazine that could move between Pole Position and the Spectrum without blinking.
items.sourceNote: Specific cover line not asserted.

items.title: Popular Computing Weekly
items.headline: Listings for the holiday machine
items.coverImageAlt: Reconstructed Popular Computing Weekly cover for December 1982.
items.curatorNote: A new micro under the television needed software, help and reassurance almost immediately.
items.body: A new micro under the television needed software, help and reassurance almost immediately.
items.sourceNote: Specific issue line not asserted.

items.title: Personal Computer World
items.headline: The computer as serious present
items.curatorNote: The respectable magazine cover helped justify the games machine hiding inside the home computer.
items.body: The respectable magazine cover helped justify the games machine hiding inside the home computer.
items.sourceNote: Specific cover line not asserted.

sections.title: Online Life
sections.subtitle: December's online life was anticipation without refresh buttons.

items.title: Wish lists were curated by paper
items.body: Magazine pages, shop catalogues and handwritten notes did the work of comparison sites.

items.title: Rumours travelled through schools
items.body: A cartridge's quality might be known through one child, one cousin, or one half-remembered shop demo.

items.title: Type-ins became holiday projects
items.body: A Christmas micro could turn a magazine listing into an afternoon of careful typing.

items.title: No patches were coming
items.body: A rushed or confusing cartridge arrived as a finished object. Players could learn it, swap it, or regret it, but not update it.

sections.title: What It Felt Like
sections.subtitle: December 1982 felt abundant before it felt ominous.

items.title: The shelf looked impossibly full
items.body: There were too many machines, too many promises and too many logos, which was thrilling until it became confusing.

items.title: E.T. carried cinema magic and risk
items.body: The licence made sense from across a shop counter; the play experience would be argued about later.

items.title: The UK micro felt like a permission slip
items.body: A computer could be defended as education, then used for games the moment homework was mentioned.

items.title: Hindsight had not arrived
items.body: The crash was not yet the everyday story. The everyday story was choosing what to ask for before the shops ran out.
